program.title: The 4-week program
program.what: A guided 4-week journey through CBT, built from the tools already in this module.
program.how: Each week pairs a recurring daily practice with a one-time milestone; do the practice across several days and the week completes itself.
program.why: Spacing practice over days builds real habits and lasting change rather than a one-off burst.

thoughtRecords.title: Thought records
thoughtRecords.what: A structured way to capture a distressing thought and examine it.
thoughtRecords.how: Note the situation and automatic thought, weigh the evidence for and against, then write a more balanced thought.
thoughtRecords.why: Putting thoughts on paper loosens their grip and trains you to question unhelpful thinking.

beliefs.title: Core beliefs
beliefs.what: Deep, long-held rules about yourself, others, or the world.
beliefs.how: Name the belief, gather evidence for and against, and craft a kinder, more accurate alternative to rehearse.
beliefs.why: Core beliefs sit beneath many negative thoughts; softening them eases problems at the root.

worry.title: Worry time
worry.what: A method for containing repetitive worry instead of letting it run all day.
worry.how: Postpone worries to a short daily worry time, then sort each into solvable (problem-solve) or hypothetical (let go).
worry.why: Worry rarely changes outcomes; scheduling it frees the rest of your day and breaks the worry habit.

distortions.title: Thinking distortions
distortions.what: Common biased thinking patterns, like catastrophising or all-or-nothing thinking.
distortions.how: Learn to spot the distortion behind a thought, then reframe it more realistically.
distortions.why: Naming the pattern makes an automatic thought easier to challenge and let go.

goals.title: Goals
goals.what: Clear, value-driven targets that give your work direction.
goals.how: Set specific 'start' goals - what you want to move toward, not just stop - and track progress.
goals.why: Concrete goals turn vague intentions into steps you can act on and measure.

values.title: Values
values.what: What matters most to you across life areas, used to guide goals and actions.
values.how: Rate how important and how satisfied you are in each life domain to spot the gaps.
values.why: Acting in line with your values builds meaning and motivation that outlast any single goal.

activities.title: Activity scheduling
activities.what: Planning meaningful activities across Physical, Achievement, Connection and Enjoyment (PACE).
activities.how: Schedule small activities, do them, and rate your mood before and after.
activities.why: Doing comes before feeling - active engagement lifts mood and breaks cycles of withdrawal.

exposure.title: Graded exposure
exposure.what: Facing feared situations step by step instead of avoiding them.
exposure.how: Build a ladder of situations by difficulty, start low, and repeat each step until the anxiety eases.
exposure.why: Repeated exposure teaches your brain the feared outcome rarely happens, normalising the anxiety response.

tasks.title: Stuck-task breakdown
tasks.what: A way to get moving on tasks you've been avoiding or putting off.
tasks.how: Break the task into the smallest next steps, name the fear behind the avoidance, and act on one step.
tasks.why: Small wins reduce avoidance and prove the task is more manageable than it felt.

anger.title: Anger log
anger.what: Tracking anger episodes to understand triggers and urges.
anger.how: Log what set it off, how intense it was, the urge you felt, and whether you took a time-out.
anger.why: Seeing your patterns helps you respond on purpose instead of reacting on impulse.

selfCare.title: Self-care check-in
selfCare.what: A daily check on the basics that protect wellbeing - sleep, movement, connection, nourishment.
selfCare.how: Log how you slept, moved, ate and connected, plus one meaningful activity or gratitude.
selfCare.why: Looking after the basics steadies mood and builds resilience against setbacks.

breathing.title: Breathing
breathing.what: Slow, abdominal breathing to calm the body.
breathing.how: Breathe gently into the belly, letting each out-breath release tension; practise a few minutes at a time.
breathing.why: Slowing the breath settles the nervous system and lowers physical arousal.

mindfulness.title: Mindfulness
mindfulness.what: Paying open, non-judgemental attention to the present moment.
mindfulness.how: Notice thoughts, feelings and sensations as they come and go, gently returning focus when the mind wanders.
mindfulness.why: Stepping back from thoughts reduces rumination and builds emotional steadiness.

meditation.title: Meditation
meditation.what: Longer, guided attention practice to train focus and calm.
meditation.how: Follow a guided session, resting attention on the breath or body and returning when distracted.
meditation.why: Regular practice strengthens attention and lowers stress over time.

grounding.title: Grounding
grounding.what: Quick techniques to anchor you in the present during distress.
grounding.how: Engage your senses - name what you can see, hear, feel and smell - to shift focus out of the mind and into the moment.
grounding.why: Grounding interrupts spirals of anxiety or overwhelm and restores a sense of safety.

mood.title: Mood tracker
mood.what: A daily log of how you feel.
mood.how: Rate your mood and note emotions and context, then review the trends over time.
mood.why: Tracking reveals patterns and links between what you do and how you feel.

sleep.title: Sleep
sleep.what: Tracking and improving the basics of restful sleep.
sleep.how: Log your sleep and review hygiene factors - wind-down routine, screens, light, caffeine.
sleep.why: Good sleep underpins mood, focus and resilience; small habit changes can make a big difference.

journal.title: Journal
journal.what: Free writing to process thoughts and feelings.
journal.how: Write openly about what's on your mind, without editing or judging.
journal.why: Putting experience into words helps you make sense of it and lowers its emotional charge.

gratitude.title: Gratitude log
gratitude.how: Record a few small or large things you appreciate, regularly.
gratitude.why: Deliberately noticing the good shifts attention away from threat and lifts mood over time.

habits.title: Habits
habits.what: Building and tracking small, repeatable routines.
habits.how: Choose a small habit, attach it to a daily cue, and check it off each day.
habits.why: Consistent small actions compound into lasting change.

defusion.title: Defusion
defusion.what: A way to step back from unhelpful thoughts and see them as mental events rather than facts.
defusion.how: Notice the thought, name it ("I'm having the thought that…"), then use a technique to create distance — labelling, singing, or watching it like a passing cloud.
defusion.why: When fused with a thought it controls your behaviour. Defusion reduces its grip without arguing, fixing, or suppressing it.

expansion.title: Expansion
expansion.what: Making room for difficult emotions and sensations instead of fighting or avoiding them.
expansion.how: Locate the feeling in your body, breathe into it, and observe it with curiosity — its shape, size, weight — rather than trying to push it away.
expansion.why: Struggling against difficult feelings amplifies them. Expanding around them turns off the fight response and lets them pass naturally.

connection.title: Connection
connection.what: Present-moment awareness — noticing what is happening right now rather than getting lost in the past or future.
connection.how: Engage your five senses to anchor attention: notice what you can see, hear, feel, smell, and taste right now.
connection.why: Most suffering happens in thoughts about past or future. Returning to the present interrupts rumination and creates a moment of calm.

observingSelf.title: Observing Self
observingSelf.what: The stable part of you that notices thoughts and feelings without being defined by them — your inner witness.
observingSelf.how: Step into the observer perspective: notice that you are the one who has thoughts, not the thoughts themselves.
observingSelf.why: Identifying with a constant observer reduces the power of fluctuating thoughts and feelings to dictate your sense of self.

committedAction.title: Committed Action
committedAction.what: Taking values-guided steps even when discomfort, fear, or difficult feelings are present.
committedAction.how: Choose a small, concrete action toward your values, commit to it, and follow through regardless of how you feel in the moment.
committedAction.why: Waiting to feel ready means waiting forever. Small committed steps build momentum and prove values-guided living is possible even in difficulty.
